skill.name: quality-fixer
skill.description: Applies Python formatting and lint auto-fixes with Black, isort, Ruff, and project quality commands. Use it to clean code style issues before checks, reviews, or commits.
skill.summary: Clean Python formatting and lint issues before quality checks.
skill.author: Atyantik

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many external command examples and two high-severity weak-crypto patterns. Review found the weak-crypto, network reconnaissance, and system reconnaissance hits are false positives in documentation text, while the command examples are expected for a Python quality-fixing workflow. The main confirmed concern is that the skill instructs agents to run workspace-modifying commands and includes a destructive git reset rollback example, so publication should include a warning.

medium_findings.title: Workspace-Modifying Quality Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally instructs agents to run formatting, linting, and git commands that can change repository files. These commands are legitimate for the stated purpose, but they can alter user work and should be run only after review or in dry-run mode when possible.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly instruct execution of formatter, linter, diff, add, and commit commands. The commands match the skill purpose, but they clearly modify the local workspace.

low_findings.title: Destructive Git Rollback Example
low_findings.description: The backup strategy recommends git reset --hard HEAD~1 as an undo option. This can discard committed and uncommitted work if used incorrectly, but it is documented as a rollback step after creating a backup commit rather than as malicious behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The destructive command is explicitly present on line 399. Context shows it is meant as a recovery option, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak-Crypto Static Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts do not correspond to cryptographic operations. The cited lines contain frontmatter description text and a limitation about design flaws, with no hashing or encryption code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact cited lines contain natural-language documentation only. No cryptographic API, algorithm selection, or digest computation appears at these locations.

low_findings.title: False Positive Reconnaissance Static Matches
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance alerts are natural-language or Python example text, not reconnaissance behavior. No evidence found of scanning hosts, enumerating system details, or sending network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation bullets and a string-formatting example. A targeted search found no curl, wget, nmap, nc, ssh, or exfiltration patterns in SKILL.md.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Execution Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly tells agents to run local quality and git commands. This is expected for the skill, but it should be treated as a permission-sensitive workflow because commands can modify files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and numerous. They are not malicious in context, but they depend on local project scripts and user review.

content.value_statement: Python projects often fail checks because formatting, imports, and safe lint fixes drift over time. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through formatter, linter, review, and verification steps.
actual_capabilities: Runs or guides Black and isort formatting workflows for Python projects.
Applies safe Ruff auto-fixes for imports and simple lint issues.
Helps resolve Black and Ruff formatting conflicts with concrete patterns.
Supports dry-run review through formatter and linter diff commands.
Reports modified files, fixed issue categories, and follow-up checks.
Encourages project documentation review before changing code.
limitations: It does not fix business logic, algorithms, or failing tests.
It depends on project tools such as make, Black, isort, Ruff, and pytest.
Some Pylint, mypy, and complex typing issues still require manual changes.
Auto-fixes can modify files, so users should review diffs before committing.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Pull Request
use_cases.description: Clean formatting, imports, and safe lint issues before asking for review.

use_cases.title: Recover From CI Quality Failures
use_cases.description: Use local quality commands to reproduce failures, apply safe fixes, and verify the result.

use_cases.title: Standardize Python Project Style
use_cases.description: Apply consistent formatting and conflict resolution across source and test files.

prompt_templates.title: Fix All Safe Issues
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply safe quality fixes to this Python project. Read the project documentation first, run the configured formatter and linter fixes, then show me the changed files and checks to run.

prompt_templates.title: Preview Changes First
prompt_templates.scenario: Dry run when changes need review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Preview quality fixes without modifying files. Show formatter and Ruff diffs, explain the likely changes, and ask before applying anything.

prompt_templates.title: Fix One File
prompt_templates.scenario: Targeted cleanup for a specific module.
prompt_templates.prompt: Fix safe formatting and lint issues only in the file I name. After the fix, show the diff summary and any remaining manual issues.

prompt_templates.title: Resolve Tool Conflicts
prompt_templates.prompt: Resolve Black and Ruff conflicts in this project. Identify the conflicting lines, apply minimal code restructuring, and verify that both tools pass.

output_examples.input: Apply safe quality fixes before I open a pull request.
output: Reads the project quality documentation and tool configuration.
Runs formatting and safe lint fixes for source and test files.
Reports changed files, categories of fixes, and verification commands.

output_examples.input: Preview what Ruff and Black would change in src/.
output: Shows a dry-run summary of formatting and lint changes.
Separates automatic fixes from items that require manual review.
Asks for confirmation before modifying files.

output_examples.input: Black and Ruff disagree on a long string.
output: Suggests extracting a message variable or splitting the string.
Verifies that formatter and linter checks agree after the change.

best_practices: Start with dry-run mode when the repository has uncommitted work.
Review every generated diff before staging or committing changes.
Run tests and quality checks after applying formatter or lint fixes.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to change business logic or algorithms.
Do not accept large auto-fix batches without reviewing the diff.
Do not run destructive git rollback commands unless the recovery target is clear.

faq.question: Does this skill write new tests?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on formatting, imports, safe lint fixes, and formatter conflicts.

faq.question: Can it fix failing application behavior?
faq.answer: No. It does not repair algorithms, business rules, or runtime defects.

faq.question: Which tools does it expect?
faq.answer: It expects common Python quality tools such as Black, isort, Ruff, make, pytest, and related checks.

faq.question: Can I preview changes before applying them?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes dry-run workflows using formatter and linter diff modes.

faq.question: Is it safe for repositories with uncommitted work?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill can modify files, so review git status and prefer dry-run mode first.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
